title: How to Buy and Sell Shares in NZ
objectives: Describe the practical steps to buy shares in NZ
Compare NZ share trading platforms and brokerage costs
Calculate total cost and return on a share trade including brokerage

vocabulary.definition: A licensed intermediary that executes share trades on your behalf. Can be a full-service broker (advice included, higher fees) or an online/discount broker (self-directed, lower fees).
The fee charged by a broker to execute a trade. NZ online brokers typically charge $10 to $30 per trade or a percentage of the transaction value.
An instruction to buy or sell immediately at whatever the current market price is. Executes instantly but price may differ slightly from what you see.
An instruction to buy or sell only at a specific price or better. Your trade will not execute unless the price reaches your limit — but you control the price you pay.
The finalisation of a share trade. In NZ, standard settlement is T+2 — the trade settles two business days after execution. Money and shares change hands on settlement day.

activities.title: Opening a Share Trading Account in NZ
activities.notePrompt: List the four things you typically need to open a share trading account in NZ. Why is a verified identity required?
activities.content:
Buying shares in NZ is much simpler than most people think. Here is the practical process.

Step 1 — Choose a platform:
— Sharesies: Popular with young NZ investors. Fractional shares available ($50 minimum). Low fees on small amounts.
— Hatch: Specialises in US shares. Flat $3 USD per trade.
— Jarden Direct (formerly ASB Securities): Established NZ broker. $30 per trade.
— Tiger Brokers: Low-cost option with access to NZ, AU and US markets.
— InvestNow: Index fund and managed fund platform — not direct share trading.

Step 2 — Open the account:
You need: NZ bank account, NZ government-issued photo ID (driver licence or passport), IRD number, and to verify your address. All platforms require Anti-Money Laundering (AML) verification under NZ law. This can be done fully online, typically in 10 to 30 minutes.

Step 3 — Fund the account:
Transfer money via internet banking. Most platforms settle funds within 1 business day.

Step 4 — Place your first order:
Search for the company by name or NZX ticker. Choose market order (buy at current price) or limit order (buy only at your specified price or lower). Confirm the trade.

activities.title: Brokerage Costs — What Trades Actually Cost
activities.notePrompt: Calculate the break-even share price movement for a $500 and a $5,000 Sharesies trade. How does brokerage change the minimum gain needed to break even?
activities.content:
Every share trade has a cost. Brokerage eats into returns, especially on small trades.

NZ online brokerage fees in 2025 (approximate):

Sharesies:
— 0.5% on NZX trades (min $0.50, max $25)
— On a $500 trade: $2.50
— On a $5,000 trade: $25 (capped)

Hatch (US shares):
— USD $3 flat fee per trade plus a small FX margin
— Good for US shares, not available for NZX

Jarden Direct:
— $30 per NZX trade (flat fee, any size)
— Better value for large trades

Break-even calculations — why trade size matters:
Sharesies, $500 trade:
Buy: $500 + $2.50 = $502.50. Sell later: brokerage another $2.50. Total cost: $5.00.
Your share must rise at least 1.0% just to break even.

Jarden Direct, $500 trade:
Buy: $500 + $30. Sell: another $30. Total cost: $60.
Your share must rise 12% just to break even.

Jarden Direct, $5,000 trade:
Buy: $5,000 + $30. Sell: another $30. Total cost: $60.
Your share must rise 1.2% to break even — now competitive.

Conclusion: flat-fee brokers are only cost-effective for larger trades. Percentage-fee platforms like Sharesies are better for smaller amounts.

activities.statement: A market order guarantees you will pay exactly the price displayed on screen when you click buy.
activities.explanation: False. A market order executes at the best available price at the moment it reaches the exchange — which may differ slightly from the price displayed when you clicked. In fast-moving markets or for less liquid shares (few buyers and sellers), the price can move between the moment you decide to buy and when the order executes. This is called slippage. A limit order solves this by only executing at your specified price or better — but it may not execute at all if the price never reaches your limit.

activities.title: Market Orders vs Limit Orders
activities.notePrompt: When would you use a market order? When would you use a limit order? Give a specific NZ example of each situation.
activities.content:
Two main ways to place a share trade order.

Market order:
— Executes immediately at the current best available price
— You get certainty of execution (the trade will definitely happen)
— But you do not control the exact price
— Best for large, liquid NZ shares (Fisher and Paykel Healthcare, Spark, Meridian) where the bid-ask spread is tiny

Limit order:
— You specify the maximum price you will pay to buy (or minimum you will accept to sell)
— Trade only executes if the market reaches your price
— You control the price but not whether the trade happens
— Best for less liquid shares with wide spreads, or when you want to buy a share that is slightly above your target price

Example — Limit order in practice:
Mainfreight is trading at $70.20. You think $68 is a fair price. You set a limit buy order at $68.00. The order sits on the exchange. If Mainfreight falls to $68 or below, your order automatically executes and you buy. If it never reaches $68, you never buy — but you also do not overpay.

For most NZ retail investors buying large NZX companies, market orders are perfectly fine. Limit orders become more important when trading smaller or less liquid companies.

questions.question: You buy 200 Spark shares at $4.20 using Sharesies (0.5% brokerage). Six months later you sell at $4.55. What is your total profit after both brokerage charges?
options: $61.25 — $70 gain minus $4.20 buy brokerage and $4.55 sell brokerage
$65.65 — $70 gain minus brokerage on buy ($4.20) and sell ($4.55)
$85.25 — including the value of dividends received
questions.explanation: Capital gain: 200 × ($4.55 − $4.20) = 200 × $0.35 = $70.00. Buy brokerage: 200 × $4.20 × 0.5% = $840 × 0.005 = $4.20. Sell brokerage: 200 × $4.55 × 0.5% = $910 × 0.005 = $4.55. Total brokerage: $4.20 + $4.55 = $8.75. Net profit: $70.00 − $8.75 = $61.25. Note: this ignores any dividends — the actual total return including dividends would be higher.

questions.question: Why is a flat-fee broker like Jarden Direct ($30 per trade) better value than Sharesies (0.5% per trade) for a $10,000 investment?
options: Flat-fee brokers always offer better customer service and more investment options
On a $10,000 trade, Sharesies charges $25 (capped) while Jarden charges $30 — they are similar, but Jarden offers more professional tools for large investors
On a $10,000 trade, Sharesies would charge $50 (0.5%) while Jarden charges $30 — Jarden is cheaper, and the advantage grows further for larger amounts above the Sharesies cap
They are identical in cost — both charge a percentage of the transaction
questions.explanation: Sharesies caps NZX brokerage at $25 (not 0.5% × $10,000 = $50 — the cap applies). So for a $10,000 trade, Sharesies charges $25 and Jarden charges $30. They are close but Jarden is slightly more expensive at this size. For trades above approximately $6,000, the Sharesies cap ($25) kicks in and remains fixed while continuing to be less than Jarden's $30. Below $6,000, Sharesies percentage fee is cheaper than Jarden's flat $30.

activities.title: Market Order or Limit Order? Match the Situation

pairs.item: Buying 500 Spark shares immediately — large liquid company, tiny bid-ask spread
You want Mainfreight but only at $65 or below — willing to wait
Selling Fisher and Paykel Healthcare today after a strong earnings report — want fast execution
Buying shares in a small NZ company with low daily trading volume — worried about wide spread

activities.explanation: Market orders prioritise speed of execution. Limit orders prioritise price control. For large, liquid NZX shares the difference is usually negligible — the spread is tiny and market orders execute close to the displayed price. For small or thinly traded companies, limit orders prevent paying significantly more than intended.

activities.statement: When you buy shares on the NZX, you receive the shares and the seller receives your money on the same day the trade executes.
activities.explanation: False. NZX trades settle on T+2 — two business days after the trade executes. On trade day you see the trade confirmed in your account, but the formal exchange of shares and cash happens two business days later. This is called settlement. You cannot sell the shares you just bought until they are settled (though some platforms show them as available immediately). Settlement delays exist to allow time for verification and administrative processing.

activities.question: You buy 100 Meridian Energy shares at $5.60 using a broker charging $30 flat. What is the total cost of this purchase including brokerage?
activities.explanation: 100 × $5.60 = $560 for the shares. Plus $30 brokerage = $590 total cost. Your average cost per share is $590 ÷ 100 = $5.90. Meridian must rise above $5.90 + another $0.30 (to cover the sell brokerage of $30 ÷ 100 shares) = $6.20 before you break even on this trade. This illustrates why brokerage costs matter — particularly for smaller trades.

activities.title: Setting Up Your First Virtual Account
activities.prompt: Go to Sharesies.nz or any NZ share trading platform and look at the account opening process. What information do they ask for? What documents do they need? How long would it take you to open an account? Write down three questions you have about the process that you would want answered before investing real money.
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — understanding the practical mechanics of investing removes the barrier that stops many people getting started.
